title: Finding draughts with a smoke pencil
subtitle: A systematic room-by-room survey to locate infiltration before you seal
excerpt: A smoke pencil reveals where air is entering a building by making airflow visible. A methodical survey on a cold, still day covers skirting boards, window reveals, loft hatches, electrical back-boxes, and any service penetrations. It takes half a day and costs nothing if you have a candle.
sourceNotes: AECB Domestic Airtightness guide. Passivhaus Trust airtightness guidance for existing buildings. Energy Saving Trust draught-proofing and airtightness resources.

glossaryTerms.definition: Uncontrolled air movement through gaps, cracks, and service penetrations in the building fabric. Distinct from deliberate ventilation through openable windows and trickle vents. In a typical pre-1940s UK house, infiltration accounts for 25-40% of total heat loss.

The resistance of the building fabric to uncontrolled air leakage. Measured in air changes per hour at 50 Pa pressure difference (ACH50) using a blower door test. UK Building Regulations require 10 m3/h/m2 at 50 Pa for new builds; Passivhaus requires 0.6 ACH50.

content.text: Smoke reveals
air infiltration

by making the airflow visible. On a cold day with at least 5°C temperature difference between inside and out, the stack effect drives cold outside air in through low gaps and warm air out through high ones. A smoke pencil or a lit stick of incense held near a suspected gap will deflect horizontally if there is airflow. The survey does not require any equipment beyond a candle or incense stick, a calm pair of hands, and a systematic approach.
Conditions for the survey
Outside temperature below 10°C and indoor heating on: the temperature difference drives the stack effect.
Low wind: wind-driven infiltration can push smoke inward from upper gaps and confuse the picture.
Close all windows and external doors before starting: you want the natural pressure difference, not wind.
What to check: a room-by-room list
Ground floor
Floorboard gaps along all edges and across the floor. Hold the smoke 20 mm above the floor. A draught from a suspended floor usually enters at the perimeter.
Behind all skirting boards: check with a hand first, then smoke at the skirting-floor junction.
Under and around all external doors including the back door, side gate door, and French doors.
Electrical back-boxes in external walls: remove the socket faceplate and hold smoke near the gap around the box. Any gap in the back of the box to the wall cavity is an infiltration path.
Upper floors and ceilings

Loft hatch perimeter: one of the largest single infiltration paths in a typical UK house. Hold smoke at the hatch edge. Also check for warm air going up past the hatch (smoke rises into the loft) rather than cold air coming down.
Light fittings that penetrate the ceiling into the loft void: recessed downlighters are a major route for heat loss via convection if the loft is not insulated at ceiling level above them.
Window reveal edges: the gap between the window frame and the structural reveal in old timber sash windows is a consistent draught path.
Service penetrations

Where any pipe or cable enters an external wall: water supply pipes, gas supply, TV and telephone cables, boiler flues (the gap around the flue liner, not the flue itself). All should be sealed with fire-rated sealant or mineral wool packing.
Extractor fan openings that have no damper: a bathroom extractor with no back-flap damper is an unobstructed hole when not running.
Recording the results

Draw a simple floor plan (or print one from a planning application if you have it) and mark each gap found with a number. Write one line per gap: location, estimated size (small 0-5 mm / medium 5-15 mm / large over 15 mm), and the fix required. This list becomes the draught-sealing work schedule.
After the survey: prioritise by size and access

The loft hatch, skirting boards, and service penetrations are usually the fastest wins: accessible, cheap to seal, and high-impact because they are high in the building where the stack effect is strongest. Window and door gaps follow. Floorboard gaps come last because they require clearing the floor covering.
Airtightness

testing with a blower door provides a quantified result (ACH50) before and after the work. It is not necessary for a domestic draught-sealing programme, but it is the only way to measure improvement objectively; energy assessors offer this service.
